title: Experiences of first-generation immigrant parents accessing early services for their autistic children in the UK
abstract: This study aims to understand the experiences of immigrant parents who have autistic children in accessing early services from the pre-diagnosis period to education. The participants of this research involve eight first-generation immigrant parents who migrated from Türkiye to the UK and have an autistic child(ren). Research data were collected using semi-structured interviews and supported by the researcher's reflective journals and documents involving medical records and Education Health and Care Plans. The data were analysed using the interpretative phenomenological analysis approach. The findings indicated that language barriers and cultural patterns of perceptual and interpersonal communication affect parental adaptation and access to early services in both positive and negative ways, with each experience reflecting a unique path. These findings have implications for policymakers and practitioners to ensure that early intervention and support services are accessible to all children and families, irrespective of their cultural background and language barriers.
text:
Experiences of first-generation immigrant parents accessing early 
services for their autistic children in the UK☆
Gamze Kaplan a,*,1
, Paulette Luff b,2
a Division of Psychology & Mental Health, The University of Manchester, UK
b School of Education, Faculty of Arts, Humanities, Education and Social Sciences, Anglia Ruskin University, UK
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Immigrant parents
Cultural diversity
Minority
Intersectionality
Autism
A B S T R A C T
This study aims to understand the experiences of immigrant parents who have autistic children in 
accessing early services from the pre-diagnosis period to education. The participants of this 
research involve eight first-generation immigrant parents who migrated from Türkiye to the UK 
and have an autistic child(ren). Research data were collected using semi-structured interviews 
and supported by the researcher’s reflective journals and documents involving medical records 
and Education Health and Care Plans. The data were analysed using the interpretative 
phenomenological analysis approach. The findings indicated that language barriers and cultural 
patterns of perceptual and interpersonal communication affect parental adaptation and access to 
early services in both positive and negative ways, with each experience reflecting a unique path. 
These findings have implications for policymakers and practitioners to ensure that early inter­
vention and support services are accessible to all children and families, irrespective of their 
cultural background and language barriers.
1. Introduction
Autism is a neurodevelopmental condition that is typically identifiable by the age of three, though unique neurodivergent features 
can be evident from early infancy (Webb & Jones, 2009). It is characterised by differences in social interaction, communication styles, 
sensory processing, and cognitive behaviours (APA, 2013). While the journey of getting a diagnosis to access available services can 
vary for each family, it may begin with the observation of the child within the family or immediate environment. Upon recognising 
autistic features, families typically reach out to healthcare professionals such as general practitioners (GPs), health visitors, or any 
other professional like Special Educational Needs Coordinators (SENCOs), to seek referrals or collaboration (NHS, 2019a). This stage is 
shaped by the interactions of the parents, healthcare professionals and educational professionals. Later, the child is referred to the 
assessment process, and it is necessary to continue the cooperation in this process and contribute to the reliability of the assessment by 
all experts who know the child. Upon the identification of the child’s condition, various support systems become available, including 
in-school support, social care, financial assistance, and well-being services offered by government bodies, local authorities, and 
charitable organisations (National Autistic Society, 2020). Families are expected to access these support systems, as recommended by 
☆This study is part of a master’s thesis and has been funded by the Republic of Türkiye Ministry of National Education.
* Correspondence to: Jean McFarlane Building, 176 Oxford Rd, Manchester M13 9PY, UK.
E-mail address: gamze.kaplan@manchester.ac.uk (G. Kaplan).

the NHS (2019b).
While the system expects parents to seek available services, they may experience various psychological reactions as described by 
Kübler-Ross’s stages of grief (2014): denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance. These stages do not necessarily need to be 
followed in a specific order, and experiencing one stage does not preclude experiencing it again. Therefore, understanding the psy­
chological processes experienced by families to ensure organised support within the early environment and support systems is crucial. 
The Children Act, (2004) mandates assessment, coordination, and service planning for the welfare of the child and family, emphasising 
the importance of a holistic approach (Article 9A, 10, Section 17). Nevertheless, parents of autistic children often experience stress (Li 
et al., 2022) and lowered quality of life (Feng et al., 2022) due to delays/ long waiting lists and when they reach the services, a lack of 
consistency and inadequacy in support services (Marsack-Topolewski & Weisz, 2020).
In addition to navigating challenges in accessing early support services and education, being an immigrant adds an extra layer to 
their experience, and this intersectionality can compound experiences of marginalization and systemic oppression, influenced by both 
their ethnic identity and the advocacy required for a neurodiverse child. A recent review by Kaplan & Celik (2023) on parents from 
minority ethnic backgrounds raising children with disabilities highlights how cultural frameworks shape how disability is interpreted; 
sometimes through notions of spiritual testing, shame, or temporary delay rather than as a long-term condition. These interpretations 
intersect with structural factors, such as limited knowledge of available services; language barriers during clinical and educational 
encounters; and economic difficulties tied to migration. Migration often disrupts extended family networks, reducing access to 
informal caregiving support, particularly from grandparents. For example, while around 80 % of grandparents are involved with the 
care of their grandchildren in the UK (Information NOW, 2024); however, immigrant parents may be away from that grandparent 
support due to distances and visa requirements, and they may need to take this responsibility on their own, while also trying to build 
new relationships with people around (Souralova, 2020). Together, these dynamics reveal how cultural meaning-making is not iso­
lated from but embedded within systemic inequalities.
Although there has been an increase in studies exploring the intersectionality of cultural and linguistic diversity with a disability, 
the experiences of immigrant parents in England, particularly in relation to neurodiversity, have yet to be fully understood. As one of 
the ethnic minority groups in England, research on Turkish parents and their children with disability is very limited (Sandhu et al., 
2017) . The migration experience of Turkish-speaking people started with Cypriot Turks in the 1940s and then continued with the 
migration of Turkish and Kurdish communities (Uysal, 2016). Currently, the number of Turkish-speaking people has reached 
approximately 500,000 (House of Commons, 2011). In this study, the term "Turkish parents" is used to describe parents who are from 
Türkiye and have migrated to England, regardless of their ethnicity including Turkish, Kurdish or Zaza origin. Despite being a 
prominent group, Turkish children in the English education system have one of the lowest success rates (Baykusoglu, 2014).

G. Kaplan and P. Luff                                                                                                                                                                                                
Research in Autism 129 (2026) 202740 
2 
Additionally, Turkish children who are second-language learners often face suspensions in special education referrals. This could be 
attributed to confusion between language learning challenges and developmental conditions, leading to delays or incorrect assess­
ments. (Essex County Council, 2011). Given these challenges and the lack of research, it is crucial to understand the experiences of this 
overlooked group to better address their needs within early support services.
The aim of this study was to address this gap by exploring the experiences of first-generation immigrant Turkish parents with 
autistic children in accessing early support services from the pre-diagnosis stage through the education process.
2. Materials and methods
2.1. Research design
This study was designed with a phenomenological research methodology, which is an approach to exploring an individual’s life 
experiences and obtaining a deep understanding of how they make sense of their experiences (Taylor et al., 2016) by focusing on the 
lived experiences of immigrant Turkish parents.
The debate is currently ongoing regarding the definition of phenomenology and which studies are phenomenological. While Spiers 
and Smith (2019) defined such studies as those that attempt to understand how their participants make sense of their experiences, van 
Manen (2017) argued that the experience itself reveals the reality of the experience, not how it is perceived by the participant. 
Meanwhile, Zahavi (2019) claimed that whether or not a piece of research is phenomenological is related to the role of the researcher.
The difference between these definitions lies in how each approach conceptualises the study of lived experience. Descriptive or 
transcendental phenomenology, as articulated by Husserl (1931), seeks to identify the essential structures of consciousness with 
approaching phenomena from a more detached, epistemological stance. In contrast, Heidegger’s (1962) hermeneutic phenomenology, 
emphasises understanding as an interpretive and embodied mode of being. For Heidegger, Dasein is always already situated in a world, 
and its understanding arises not from abstract reflection but from lived, embodied engagement with its environment. The researcher, 
as a being-in-the-world, cannot bracket off their context but instead brings a pre-understanding shaped by their own history and 
experience. This study therefore adopts a hermeneutic phenomenological approach, recognising the interpretive potential of the re­
searcher’s shared cultural, linguistic, and embodied experience as an immigrant. Rather than a source of bias, it is understood as a 
meaningful ground for interpreting the lived experiences of participants.
Yin (2013) described the use of a well-known research technique as one of the ways to ensure trustworthiness. The researcher 
previously participated in post-graduate lectures on scientific research methods and qualitative research methods and had experience 
with qualitative research conducted through document analysis but had no experience using interviews as the main data collection 
method. To eliminate this limitation, regular meetings with the supervisor were conducted, expert opinions on the development of the 
interview questions were consulted, and at each step of the analysis, cooperation proceeded with the supervisor.
2.2. Participants
Parents were eligible for the study if they (a) were first-generation immigrants born in Türkiye and (b) had an autistic child who had 
been diagnosed in England to understand the intersection of the experiences of migration and having an autistic child. Eight parents 
who agreed to participate in the study were recruited via Turkish family social media groups and snowballing. An exemption was made 
for a mother, who was born in England, was recruited because she was willing to participate as the partner of the father participant and 
had shared experiences with him.
Table 1 provided some descriptive information about each of the participant parents, who consist of seven mothers and one father. 
The families migrated to England after 2005 and now reside in various cities.
2.3. Data collection
The research started after the approval of the [name of the university was deleted for blind review] School of Education and Social Care 
Research Ethics Panel [reference number deleted for blind review]. The study used semi-structured interviews as the primary data

Research in Autism 129 (2026) 202740 
3 
collection technique, supported by the researcher’s reflective journals and documents involving medical records and Education, Health 
and Care Plans (EHCP), which were voluntarily shared by four parents. The researcher prepared the topic guide, and three different 
expert views were taken from experienced qualitative researchers. A pilot interview was conducted to finalise the topic guide (final 
version presented in Table 2). The informed consent was obtained both in written and verbal form, and participants were informed 
about anonymisation, their option to pause or stop the interview, and their right to withdraw the data. The interviews were conducted 
and transcribed with anonymisation by the primary researcher. Participants were given the option to choose between speaking Turkish 
or English, all preferred Turkish. The interviews were conducted over Zoom, lasting between 42 and 82 min, with permission for 
recordings granted.
During the interviews, parents had no issues disclosing their experiences and feelings, and they shared sensitive moments, such as 
crying. To manage emotional intensity, participants were reminded from time to time that they could take a break at any time.
2.4. Data analysis
In this study, Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA) was used, which is a strongly idiographic approach that focuses on 
detailed analysis of the case either as an end in itself or before moving to similarly detailed analyses of other cases (Smith et al., 2009). 
The analysis involved several steps, such as meaning-making, uncovering emergent themes, creating individual and group tables of 
themes, and writing up (Spiers & Smith, 2019). Each interview was analysed separately and completed before moving on to the next, 
ensuring an idiographic commitment to the data (ibid). The emergent themes were produced by further evaluation and 
cross-examination of similarities and differences among the sub-codes. Finally, the parent superordinate themes were identified to 
address the research questions.
Once the interpretive themes were identified, the findings were then examined through a conceptual lens. Regarding the use of 
theory in phenomenological research, van Manen (2014) states that theory may be applied when it meaningfully intersects with 
research findings. In this study, Bronfenbrenner’s (2006) bioecological theory was brought in after the analysis, as the themes pointed 
to different layers of influence that is ranging from immediate environments like family and services to broader social and cultural 
contexts. The theory provided a useful structure for presenting and situating the findings, without shaping the initial process of 
interpretation.
3. Theory
Bronfenbrenner’s ecological theory (1979) emphasises that a child’s development is not only influenced by the child but also by the 
systems around them. They proposed four subsystems that are closely and distantly connected to the child and highlighted the 
importance of understanding the relationships between these systems. These subsystems are: (a) microsystem, the immediate envi­
ronment where a child spends regular time, such as parents, teachers, and friends; (b) mesosystem, the relationship between two or 
more microsystems (e.g., family experiences affecting school relations); (c) exosystem, the external environment that is not directly 
related to the child, but the decisions taken and events occurring there affect the child and their microsystems such as educational 
policies; and (d) macrosystem, the broader cultural context. The theory was later updated to bioecological systems theory, which in­
cludes biological factors and the "chronosystem," which emphasises the interaction between ecological systems over time 
(Bronfenbrenner & Morris, 2006).
4. Findings and interpretation
The findings were presented within the framework of Bronfenbrenner’s (2006) bio-ecological systems and discussed in reference to 
the relevant literature and within the research scope. The themes identified were given in Fig. 2 and organised according to the 
different ecological systems; however, the primary emphasis was placed on the microsystem and mesosystem levels, as these layers 
most directly impact the daily experiences of the child and the parents.
Table 2 
Semi-structured interview topic guide.
• Could you start by telling us a bit about yourself and your child, so we can get to know you better?
• At what point did you begin to notice any differences in your child’s development, and what signs stood out to you?
• Can you describe the journey leading up to your child’s diagnosis of special needs?
• What was your experience like during your child’s diagnostic process?
• After receiving the official referral, could you share your experience with the decision-making process around your child’s education?
• What has your experience been like throughout your child’s educational journey?
• Looking back on the entire process, what were your expectations of the people or institutions involved? Were those expectations met?
• In your view, how has cultural diversity influenced your experience during this process?
• How has having a child with special needs affected your daily life, including family dynamics, social relationships, and work life?
• What advice or suggestions would you give to other families who are just beginning this journey?
• Is there anything else you would like to share with us?
* The term "special needs" was deliberately used during the interviews to align with the terminology commonly used within the community and to 
minimise potential disruption to the natural flow of conversation.
G. Kaplan and P. Luff                                                                                                                                                                                                
Research in Autism 129 (2026) 202740 
4 
4.1. Micro-system – child-related findings - experiencing the journey of recognition
Although this study focused on parental experiences, the child was positioned at the core of the bio-ecological systems theory, with 
parents considered part of the microsystem, as all findings were reflected through the parental perspective with a focus on the child. 
This section presents findings related to the child, particularly their early and accompanying features, which formed the basis for 
accessing early support services.
4.1.1. Initial features
Parents reported early features of autism in their children, such as a lack of response to their names, reduced eye contact, repetitive 
behaviours/stims, and delayed motor development. One mother noted: "When I called my daughter’s name, there was no reaction. She 
stopped making eye contact, and repetitive behaviours became frequent." Another mother described her son: "He stopped listening and 
responding to us, not using his pointing finger, as if he was shutting down into his own world."
Parents described a range of accompanying features they observed in their children during the early years. One mother encap­
sulated the sleep issues by stating, “The biggest problems of these children are both not being able to sleep at night and waking up at night”. 
Hyperactivity was also noted by several participants. One parent described their child’s behaviour as "The hard thing is not that he is 
autistic, but that he is hyperactive with autism. He doesn’t stop for 5 s." Moreover, the children reportedly had eating issues such as 
sensitivity to textures, portion control challenges, and aversions to certain types of food. For example, a mother said, “They don’t like 
juicy foods very much.”, illustrating a reluctance to eat foods with certain textures, a common manifestation of sensory-based food 
selectivity.
4.2. Microsystem - parent-related findings - acceptance, resilience and growth through adversity
In addition to the child-related findings, parent-related findings have been located in the microsystem, and the findings were 
related to the psychological experiences of parents presented in this section, including recognition, acceptance, research, and life 
experiences.
4.2.1. Recognising early developmental features
Parents recognised early signs of autism at varying points, ranging from two months of age to school age. The differences in the 
child were primarily noticed by the mother, although sometimes aunts, grandmothers, or parents of other children may also observe 
them. For instance, a father recounted, “My sister told me. When I went to Türkiye, my sister went to a doctor there and made an 
appointment”, while a mother explained, “I told my mother about my suspicions regarding [child]’s autism. My mother agreed, saying, ‘I think 
Fig. 2. The findings within the framework of the bio-ecological systems theory.
G. Kaplan and P. Luff                                                                                                                                                                                                
Research in Autism 129 (2026) 202740 
5 
so too’.”, similar to findings by Sicherman et al. (2018), where grandmothers were often the first to notice features. However, variations 
exist, as one mother described, “We are still discussing with my mother in Turkey. For example, for my mother, the child has nothing, I label 
the child myself”, aligning with the findings of Habayeb et al. (2020), who stated that grandparents can provide conflicting opinions and 
advice about the child’s condition, which may influence the parents’ relationship with the wider family, leading them to either hide the 
child’s condition or decrease the amount of communication with them.
Initially, parents often denied or did not realise the autistic features, often normalising their child’s behaviour as part of typical 
development, attributing it to a lack of awareness about autism, the absence of expert guidance, limited opportunities to compare their 
child with peers, and common beliefs, such as the idea that boys tend to speak later. Rather than suspecting autism, they often 
considered other explanations, including hearing impairment, speech and language delays, or attention-related difficulties. A mother 
expressed this uncertainty, saying, “There was something, I felt something, but I didn’t know what. Doesn’t he hear? Is that why he’s so 
grumpy?” Concerns about hearing were particularly common. Parents frequently mentioned that hearing assessments were conducted 
during the diagnostic process, and audiology results were often included in reports shared by parents. This aligns with findings of Sousa 
et al. (2009) reported that 62 % of 54 autistic children initially raised concerns about hearing loss. Delays in speech and language 
development were also attributed by parents to factors such as bilingualism at home, limited family social interaction, or changes 
following the arrival of a sibling. As one mother reflected, “He became a brother. He had a sister when he was 10–16 months old. I said, you 
know, maybe a situation of jealousy occurred.”
Families with prior knowledge about autism or child development, or those who have previously had a child diagnosed with 
autism, tended to recognise neurodiverse features and seek diagnosis earlier. Only one participant mentioned having prior knowledge 
about autism and child development, emphasising personal interest in the subject: “I also knew a little bit about autism before. This is how 
I had read things about children, both necessary and unnecessary information.”
4.2.2. Acceptance
The arrival of an autistic child brings about a new and unfamiliar change in a parent’s life. Access to appropriate services for the 
child involves the parents’ acceptance and adaptation. Our findings indicated that parents’ adaptation to the diagnosis aligned with the 
stages of grief outlined by Kübler-Ross and Kessler (2014). Parents reported beginning with shock, progressing to denial for some, and 
experiencing depression for others. A mother vividly expressed her initial feelings: “It was very difficult. I could not accept it. I could not 
sleep. I cried whenever I saw him.” Over time, after a prolonged period of uncertainty, parents became more certain of their recognition of 
the situation and prepared themselves for the caregiving process. Additionally, responses to the diagnosis, such as gratitude, resig­
nation, and viewing the situation as a test from a spiritual perspective, appeared to support their adaptation process. This fatalistic 
approach can serve as coping resources for families, offering a framework for meaning-making in the context of their child’s diagnosis 
(Heer et al., 2012; Meiza et al., 2019).
Research suggests that fathers typically follow a different acceptance path than mothers (Legg & Tickle, 2019). One mother 
described the father’s process as more subdued: “His shock was a little quieter. I was more active, so I was doing something, crying, or 
searching. So, I was doing something”. Two mothers emphasised that acceptance was primarily their responsibility, and mothers tended 
to be more solution-oriented, deeming it necessary for the benefit of the child. These gendered dynamics, however, were not always 
harmonious; differences in coping styles sometimes led to family conflict and, in more severe cases, marital breakdown. Prior research 
has linked fathers’ quieter responses to feelings of exclusion during the assessment process, highlighting the need for more inclusive 
and gender-sensitive support (Legg & Tickle, 2019). While cultural factors were not explicitly discussed by our participants, our 
findings may also speak to broader cultural expectations around gender roles in parenting. As Lashewicz et al. (2019) argue, fathers 
from diverse cultural backgrounds often navigate additional layers of complexity; including language barriers, cultural norms, and 
stereotypes, which can influence how they experience and respond to a diagnosis of autism.
4.2.3. Parents’ life experiences
The experiences of parents with autistic children highlight significant life changes and their impact. Many parents struggled to 
recall past experiences, focusing instead on the future - likely due to shock stages during adaptation, which can create ’shock memory.’ 
One mother described how her child’s autism reshaped every aspect of her life, leading to reprioritisation and reduced tolerance. 
Another parent emphasised the physical exhaustion from caregiving. Studies have shown that parental stress decreases when they are 
able to fulfil their parenting roles (Hou et al., 2023), underscoring the importance of addressing parental well-being.
The absence of extended family deprived parents of crucial social support in their daily lives, as explained by one of the mothers 
“Their grandmother should look after them. Let me go and rest for a couple of hours and relax a bit. There is no such thing”. While the culture of 
babysitting is growing in Türkiye and the Turkish communities (˙Inan & Do˘gan-Temur, 2010), the financial burden and the lack of trust 
in strangers often discourage parents (Sarıtas¸, 2020). This reliance on parental care further isolated mothers, limiting their ability to 
work or engage socially. Particularly, single mothers faced overwhelming challenges, often feeling they had no life beyond caregiving, 
consistent with Copeland and Harbaugh’s (2005) findings that single parents may experience high levels of anxiety and stress due to 
the lack of partner support.
Additionally, parents often felt a need to hide their child’s condition from the broader community, leading to changes in their social 
relationships: “They didn’t know my child’s diagnosis. I didn’t want them to see that there was a difference in the child”. This perceived 
stigma can contribute to social isolation, as families of autistic children may stay away from the community due to sensitivity to others’ 
reactions (Kearney & Griffin, 2001). Despite maintaining relationships with other families of children with autism, parents may 
struggle to navigate social interactions outside of this circle.
G. Kaplan and P. Luff                                                                                                                                                                                                
Research in Autism 129 (2026) 202740 
6 
4.3. Mesosystem - navigating the complexities of accessing support services
Findings concerning stakeholders beyond the parent, such as the school, peers, and immediate environment, were situated in the 
mesosystem. as they reflected parental interactions with these systems rather than the child’s direct experiences, highlighting the 
crucial role of collaboration with others in accessing services.
4.3.1. Assessment experiences
Three parents encountered health issues during the prenatal period, leading to either pre-term or late births. Expectant mothers at 
risk of pre-term birth typically undergo intensive monitoring until delivery; however, this level of monitoring may not always be 
provided for post-term births. For example, one mother shared her experience of going through a late birth, detailing the lack of 
support until the bleeding began, and highlighted the negative psychological impact of such experiences on their mental health. A 
meta-analysis aimed to identify perinatal factors related to autism has indicated a correlation between pre-term birth and an increased 
likelihood of autism (Laverty et al., 2021). However, no established link has been found between post-term birth and autism. 
Nonetheless, pregnancy complications can significantly influence parental mental health and the child’s developmental trajectories 
(Lahti et al., 2015). Thus, follow-up care could be essential to focus not only on the risk of pre-term birth but also on other 
complications.
Parents of autistic children can use various paths to seek assessment referrals, such as contacting their General Practitioner (GP), 
speech and language therapists, or charities. However, the application and referral process can be notably lengthy in the UK, with an 
average delay of approximately 3.5 years from the initial concern to the confirmation of autism (Crane et al., 2016). In Türkiye, as­
sessments are typically conducted by child psychiatrists or paediatricians in public or private hospitals. The timeframe in Türkiye tends 
to be shorter than in the UK, between the initial concern and diagnosis, around 1–1.5 years (Yaylaci & Guller, 2023). As illustrated by 
one mother, the autistic features of their child were first noticed during a visit to Türkiye, where they had an initial assessment. 
However, upon returning to England, they faced delays in obtaining a diagnosis. Expressing frustration, the mother stated, "[we] made 
an appointment with the GP. They said he was too young to be diagnosed and scheduled an appointment six months later. I did not want to wait 
and went back to Türkiye."
4.3.2. Access to services
Parents of autistic children may require access to many different services based on their unique needs. This study’s participants’ 
experience of accessing the necessary services for themselves or their children were examined under three categories: educational, 
psychological and alternative services.
4.3.2.1. Educational services. The formal educational support received by the participants included various services, such as school 
attendance, group-based speech and language therapy, occupational therapy, and sensory integration therapy. Additionally, they 
benefited from one-to-one classroom support, family training, and charity support programs.
Some parents sought private educational services, such as one-to-one therapies at home or private institutions, including speech 
and language therapy and Applied Behaviour Analysis (ABA) therapy. Due to low expectations from school support, parents turned to 
home-based sessions to meet their children’s basic educational needs. One mother expressed optimism about ABA, stating “[name] is a 
kid who can move forward very well with ABA, because he is a child who likes working at the desk, with order and discipline.”. However, 
recently, research and practice have increasingly shifted toward naturalistic approaches as behaviouristic methods and teaching so­
cially desirable behaviours have become less accepted. Particularly within the neurodiversity movement, autistic advocates argue that 
such methods can undermine self-identity, encourage masking, and negatively affect mental health (Bernardin et al., 2021; Milton, 
2018). Further research into how families perceive ABA therapy and why they seek private services would be valuable.
During the transition to school, children’s individual differences become apparent, some adapt easily, while others need more time. 
Facilitating smooth transitions was a policy challenge (OECD, 2006), requiring collaboration between schools, preschools, families, 
and local communities (Hugo et al., 2018). One mother emphasised strong support: “Because I don’t speak much English, they knew that I 
could not do it [alone], they supported me a lot. Both the school pedagogue, an extra translator, and a teacher from the school came to school 
visits with me”. In contrast, two mothers found that their schools were unwilling to cooperate, excluding families from the transition 
process and depriving their children of the education they deserved, highlighting the importance of schools building strong re­
lationships and communication with parents, as highlighted by Falkmer et al. (2015). Some parents reported gaps in planning, 
affecting their children’s placement options, “In none of the annual reviews, not even the educational psychologist came and wrote a report 
again. The reports are still from the kindergarten. When we apply to another mainstream school with that report, they tell us this kid needs a lot 
of help; we can’t get them”, despite guidelines stating that a child’s EHCPs should be reviewed annually with family participation 
(Department for Education and Department of Health and Social Care, 2015).
4.3.2.2. Psychological services. All parents emphasised the need for psychological support throughout the process. However, lacking 
guidance, some sought support independently, which they found to be crucial and helpful. Additionally, one mother expressed her 
intention to schedule an appointment with the NHS for this purpose. Yet, she felt pessimistic about the support she would receive due 
to difficulties in making appointments, long waiting times, and the language barrier.
4.3.2.3. Alternative services. Parents often explored alternative approaches such as diet modifications and supplements under a 
G. Kaplan and P. Luff                                                                                                                                                                                                
Research in Autism 129 (2026) 202740 
7 
consultant’s supervision, particularly due to the lack of support for these practices within the UK healthcare system; they often obtain 
medical prescriptions and appointments in Türkiye. Harrington et al. (2006) noted that long waiting times for traditional healthcare 
services diminished parents’ trust in healthcare professionals, leading them to seek alternative practices without scientific basis. This 
was reflected in researcher reflective journal, "Many different alternative applications are discussed in social media groups. An active person 
in the group mentions conspiracy theories, stating that scientists should not be trusted. Parents respect her very much" referring to an individual 
advocating for alternative practices.
4.4. Exosystem - Navigating legal regulations and official procedures
Legal regulations play a pivotal role in shaping service delivery and establishing the overall framework. While early intervention, 
defined as timely support to reduce the risk of poor outcomes and expected to begin as soon as developmental concerns arise (Dorset 
County Hospital NHS Foundation Trust, 2022), does not require a formal diagnosis and should be initiated through the graduated 
‘Assess, Plan, Do, Review’ approach within early years settings or at home (SEND Code of Practice, 2015), participants reported 
receiving no such support prior to diagnosis.
Access to specialist services typically requires documented developmental delay or formal assessment. For instance, referrals to 
speech and language therapy are generally accepted after a period in which universal support strategies have not adequately met the 
child’s needs (WSFT, 2025). As a result, delays in obtaining a diagnosis, often beyond age three due to long waiting times or eligibility 
thresholds, meant many families missed out on both early and specialist support during a critical developmental period. Indeed, 
research by Crane et al. (2016) and Smith-Young et al.(2020) underscored the prolonged period between parents first noticing their 
child may need support and accessing the relevant services. One mother described this situation as a "war", highlighting the challenge 
of accessing appropriate services. This gap between policy and practice represents a substantial barrier to timely intervention, 
particularly in the 0–3-year period, which is widely recognised as crucial for improving parent–child interaction and developmental 
trajectories (Green et al., 2017).
Moreover, one mother reported that while a teaching assistant was assigned to support her child, the assistant’s attention was 
divided due to the needs of many other students. Despite the SEND Code of Practice (2015) requiring schools to provide support for 
every child in need, the mother felt there was insufficient help for both her child and others. Although second- and third-level support 
can be offered based on individual needs, challenges may arise in accurately assessing and meeting those needs during 
implementation.
4.5. Macrosystem - Cultural perspectives and coping strategies
Culture, perception and belief systems shape individuals’ attitudes and behaviours, and in this study, the experiences of immigrant 
parents were examined through the lens of how their cultural frameworks and diverse backgrounds influenced their interpretations 
and daily lives.
4.5.1. Conceptualisation of autism
When discussing the concept of autism with the participants, four parents referred to it as a "disease," and one defined it as "a child 
who cannot express himself." Another mother described autism within the framework of “normality” due to her child not exhibiting 
any physical and cognitive differences. These interpretations may reflect the influence of ableism, which can be defined as a system of 
beliefs and practices that position non-disabled bodies and minds as inherently more valuable, often reinforcing social hierarchies 
based on ability (More, 2023). In the Turkish context, ableism operates through internalised social norms that idealise physical and 
cognitive “normality,” often marginalising those who deviate from these standards (Ünal, 2018). This cultural framing may shape how 
families understand autism; not as a form of neurodiversity, but as a deviation from what is socially accepted.
Beliefs are central components in informing a parent’s attitude and approach to their child’s condition. Regarding those core 
beliefs, one mother explained that her child “is not in a position to understand evil. How happy for you! Just think about it: a living thing you 
give birth to that will not harm the world.” Kennett (2002) refers to this type of belief as an expression of “attractive moral innocence”, a 
framing in which the child is seen as embodying a kind of moral purity, untainted by deception, cruelty, or malice. Although this 
innocence is distinguished from full moral agency, the belief itself can be deeply meaningful for parents and may offer a sense of 
reassurance. Additionally, some believed that a child’s disability was a punishment from God for sins committed. This notion is 
primarily rooted in belief systems and is consistent with findings from a study conducted by Treloar (2002).
4.5.2. The influence of cultural and linguistic diversity
The influence of culturally and linguistically diverse backgrounds was evident in various aspects of supporting the development of 
the child, family relationships, and interactions with other stakeholders and society at large.
Parents generally found those they interacted with to be friendly and accepting of autism, with high levels of communication and 
social acceptance. However, they often doubted the sincerity of some individuals due to misinformation from teachers about entitled 
services. Additionally, the facial expressions of those they discussed their child’s condition with sometimes revealed a simplified 
understanding of the situation: “When something happens to someone else’s child, our eyes fill with tears. But here, everything is minimised. 
Like, ’Your child is autistic, just accept it,’ with a smile, as if it’s nothing serious.”
Parents utilised the advantage of their diverse cultural backgrounds and language abilities to access support services and resources 
both in their current and home countries. However, language barriers remained significant despite interpreter support, leading to the 
G. Kaplan and P. Luff                                                                                                                                                                                                
Research in Autism 129 (2026) 202740 
8 
need for intermediaries in communication. The EMTAS Project (2011) highlighted another consequence of the language barrier: 
experts, teachers, and parents often postpone the application or referral process due to lower progress in the child’s language 
development than their peers. The complex layers of the intersection of having a neurodiverse child and speaking another language 
have been exemplified by some parents. They stopped speaking their mother tongue with their children out of fear of confusing them, 
despite recommendations to continue its use: “If other families are bilingual, they shouldn’t give up their language, because I can see now that 
my son actually had that capacity. He could learn two languages… I want to return to [using] Turkish.”
4.6. Chronosystem - Changes over time
The Chronosystem reflects changes in situations and mentalities over time. Participants reported initially feeling alienated by their 
circumstances, with a mother describing an inability to enjoy time with their child at the beginning. However, this perception changed 
over time: “When they said that … you will have a lot of fun when spending time with him, I said to my partner, ‘Look at these people: cold 
comfort’, [but] I am having fun with [name] right now. I’m really having fun … But this was a ridiculous thought for me two years ago.”
Furthermore, participants described a shift in their perception of their child as their development progressed. As they learned 
effective interaction and communication strategies, their understanding deepened. A father highlighted the need for adaptation due to 
cultural and temporal shifts in child-rearing practices. The arrival of an autistic child prompted a lifestyle adjustment, initially pre­
senting an unfamiliar situation. However, over time, they found a new balance in their lives.
5. Limitations and recommendations
This study focused on first-generation immigrant parents from Türkiye, which limits insight into how experiences and beliefs may 
shift across generations. Future research could include second- and third-generation parents to explore whether cultural in­
terpretations of autism, such as viewing it as destiny or through an ableist lens, persist over time. Also, longitudinal qualitative studies 
would help to understand how parental decisions and interpretations evolve and how these shape children’s long-term outcomes.
The study primarily examined cultural background, with less attention to intersecting factors. Future work should consider vari­
ables such as socioeconomic status, language proficiency, and discrimination to better understand the layered experiences of immi­
grant families.
When presenting the findings, direct quotations have been translated from Turkish to English for the benefit of readers. To ensure 
accuracy, these translations were then back-translated into Turkish, and at each stage, the translations were reviewed and confirmed 
by a native speaker of the respective language. However, participants and first language readers might prefer to read in Turkish due to 
potential differences in expressions and nuances best captured in their first language. In future publications intended for an inter­
national audience, including bilingual quotations can be considered.
6. Conclusion
This study explored how first-generation Turkish immigrant parents navigated early support services from the stage of initial 
concerns through the education process. Early signs were often misinterpreted or downplayed, shaped by cultural expectations, limited 
awareness, and delayed professional input. Mothers typically led the emotional and practical adaptation in the absence of extended 
family support. As they progressed, parents developed coping strategies and sought meaning through spiritual beliefs and deepening 
emotional connection, highlighting the need for early, culturally responsive support throughout the diagnostic and educational 
journey.
